questions.q: A school's IT department is planning to connect all computers within one building to share printers and files. Which type of network are they creating?
questions.explain: A LAN, or local area network, connects devices within a limited geographic area such as one building or campus wing.
optionExplanations: A WAN covers large geographic areas and multiple sites.
A LAN connects devices within a limited local area such as a building.
A VPN is an encrypted tunnel over another network, not the physical network type here.
A MAN covers a metropolitan area and is larger than a single-building network.

questions.q: A company needs a network that covers multiple cities. Which network type is most appropriate?
questions.explain: A WAN, or wide area network, is designed to connect networks across cities, states, or countries.
optionExplanations: A LAN is limited to a small local area.
A MAN usually spans a city or metro region, not multiple distant cities.
A WAN connects networks over broad geographic distances, including multiple cities.
A PAN is a personal area network for short-range personal devices.

questions.q: A marketing employee connects their wireless headphones to a laptop via Bluetooth. This is an example of a:
questions.explain: A PAN, or personal area network, links personal devices over a very short range, often using Bluetooth.
optionExplanations: A MAN spans a metropolitan area.
A PAN connects personal devices at short range such as Bluetooth peripherals.
A LAN covers a room, office, or building network.
A WAN spans large geographic areas.

questions.q: Which network component acts as a central point for connecting multiple devices in a star topology?
questions.explain: In a star topology, end devices connect back to a central switching device that forwards traffic between ports.
optionExplanations: A switch commonly serves as the central connection point in a star topology.
A modem connects a local network to an ISP service.
A firewall filters traffic based on security rules.
A NIC is an adapter inside a device, not the topology's central point.

questions.q: A law firm requires a private, encrypted tunnel over the public internet to connect its offices. Which solution should they implement?
questions.explain: A VPN creates an encrypted tunnel across a public network so remote offices can communicate securely.
optionExplanations: A MAN describes geographic scope, not the encrypted tunneling method.
A VPN provides encrypted communication over the public internet between sites.
A VLAN segments local switch traffic logically but does not create an internet tunnel by itself.
An intranet is a private internal service environment, not specifically an encrypted tunnel.

questions.q: A new hire at a company asks, 'What's the main purpose of an IP address?' Which is the correct answer?
options: To uniquely identify a device on a network
questions.explain: An IP address provides logical addressing so devices can be identified and reached on an IP network.
optionExplanations: Data storage is handled by storage devices and file systems, not IP addresses.
An IP address uniquely identifies a device interface on an IP network for communication.
Encryption protects confidentiality, which is a different function.
Blocking malware is done by security tools, not by IP addressing itself.

questions.q: Which device connects different networks and routes packets between them?
questions.explain: A router forwards packets between different IP networks using routing tables and network-layer logic.
optionExplanations: A router connects networks and routes packets between them.
A switch forwards frames primarily within the same LAN.
An access point bridges wireless clients onto a LAN.
A repeater regenerates a signal but does not route packets.

questions.q: In a home network, which device is typically used to connect to the internet through an ISP?
questions.explain: A modem interfaces with the ISP's service medium, such as cable, DSL, or fiber ONT handoff, to provide internet connectivity.
optionExplanations: A switch expands local wired ports but does not terminate the ISP line.
A router distributes traffic on the local network but commonly sits behind the modem or gateway.
A modem is typically used to connect the home to the ISP service.
A hub is an outdated shared-medium device and not the standard ISP connection device.

questions.q: A retail store needs to segment network traffic to improve performance. Which technology can accomplish this?
questions.explain: A VLAN creates logical network segments on switching infrastructure, reducing broadcast scope and separating traffic types.
optionExplanations: DNS resolves names to IP addresses and does not segment traffic.
A VLAN logically separates traffic into distinct broadcast domains.
A VPN encrypts traffic over untrusted networks rather than segmenting a LAN.
HTTP is an application protocol for web traffic.

questions.q: A company's network administrator is tasked with reducing network congestion. Which upgrade is most likely to help?
questions.explain: Switches send frames only to the destination port, unlike hubs that repeat traffic to all ports, so switching reduces unnecessary traffic and collisions.
optionExplanations: Replacing hubs with switches reduces unnecessary traffic and improves efficiency.
Adding more hubs increases shared collision domains and often worsens congestion.
Disabling encryption is not a sound congestion-management strategy and weakens security.
Cable length is not the main cause of congestion in this scenario.

questions.q: In which topology is every device connected to a single central cable?
questions.explain: A bus topology uses one main backbone cable to which devices attach.
optionExplanations: A star topology uses a central device, not a central cable backbone.
A bus topology connects devices to a single shared backbone cable.
A ring topology connects devices in a closed loop.
A mesh topology uses many interconnections between devices.

questions.q: A design team requires redundancy in their network so if one path fails, traffic can still flow. Which topology is best?
questions.explain: A mesh topology provides multiple possible paths between nodes, which increases redundancy and fault tolerance.
optionExplanations: A bus topology has a vulnerable shared backbone.
A star topology depends heavily on the central device.
A mesh topology provides multiple paths so communication can continue if one path fails.
A ring can provide some resilience in certain implementations, but full mesh offers the greatest path redundancy here.

questions.q: In a hybrid topology, what is combined?
questions.explain: A hybrid topology combines elements of two or more basic topologies, such as star-bus or star-ring.
optionExplanations: Protocols are communication rules, not topology combinations.
A hybrid topology combines two or more different topologies in one design.
Wired and wireless can coexist, but that alone does not define hybrid topology.
Using multiple ISPs is a connectivity choice, not a topology definition.

questions.q: Which topology uses a token-passing method to manage access to the network?
questions.explain: Classical ring networks are associated with token passing to control which node may transmit.
optionExplanations: Star Ethernet does not inherently use token passing.
Ring topology is historically associated with token-passing access control.
Mesh describes connectivity redundancy, not token-passing access control.
Bus Ethernet historically used CSMA/CD rather than token passing.

questions.q: In a client-server architecture, what role does the client play?
questions.explain: In client-server computing, the client initiates requests and consumes services provided by servers.
optionExplanations: Managing shared resources is typically a server or administrator role.
Providing services is the server's role in this model.
The client requests and uses services from a server.
Routing packets is a router function.

questions.q: A peer-to-peer network is most appropriate for:
questions.explain: Peer-to-peer networks fit smaller environments where devices share resources directly without dedicated centralized servers.
optionExplanations: Large corporations usually need centralized management and scalability.
Small home networks can effectively use peer-to-peer sharing.
Data centers rely on structured server-based architectures.
Global ISPs require carrier-grade network designs, not peer-to-peer LANs.

questions.q: What is a major drawback of a bus topology?
options: Single point of failure along the backbone
questions.explain: The shared backbone is critical in a bus topology, so damage to it can disrupt the entire network segment.
optionExplanations: Bus topology is usually not known for especially high cable cost.
Devices can often be added, though with practical limitations.
The backbone cable is a single point of failure in bus topology.
Bus topology is comparatively simple rather than especially complex.

questions.q: A topology where devices are connected to a central switch is called:
questions.explain: When all endpoints connect back to one central switch, the network uses a star topology.
optionExplanations: Mesh has many direct interconnections between nodes.
Star topology uses a central device such as a switch.
Ring connects devices in a loop.
Hybrid combines multiple topologies rather than this single pattern alone.

questions.q: Which topology is most commonly used in modern Ethernet LANs?
questions.explain: Modern Ethernet LANs are typically implemented as star topologies with switches at the center.
optionExplanations: Star is the standard physical topology for most modern switched Ethernet LANs.
Bus is largely obsolete for modern Ethernet deployments.
Mesh is used selectively, not as the most common Ethernet LAN topology.
Ring is uncommon in modern Ethernet LANs.

questions.q: Which architecture allows multiple clients to share files without needing a centralized server?
questions.explain: Peer-to-peer architecture allows devices to share resources directly with one another without a dedicated central server.
optionExplanations: Cloud services still rely on server infrastructure.
Client-server explicitly depends on a centralized server role.
Peer-to-peer enables direct resource sharing among participating devices.
Virtual is not a specific file-sharing architecture in this context.

questions.q: A hacker sends an overwhelming number of requests to a server, causing it to crash. What type of attack is this?
questions.explain: A distributed denial-of-service attack floods a target with traffic or requests to exhaust resources and disrupt service availability.
optionExplanations: Phishing tricks users into revealing information.
A DDoS attack overwhelms a target with traffic or requests to deny service.
A man-in-the-middle attack intercepts communications.
Spoofing falsifies identity information such as addresses or sender details.

questions.q: An employee receives an email that appears to be from the company's CEO asking for wire transfer details. This is an example of:
questions.explain: This is phishing, specifically a social engineering attempt to trick the recipient into revealing sensitive information or taking an unauthorized action.
optionExplanations: Phishing uses deceptive messages to trick a target into disclosing information or acting improperly.
Brute force involves repeated guessing, often of passwords.
SQL injection targets database queries in vulnerable applications.
Denial-of-service aims to disrupt service availability, not deceive an employee by email.

questions.q: Which protocol provides secure web communication by encrypting data?
questions.explain: HTTPS is HTTP layered over TLS, providing encryption and server authentication for web traffic.
optionExplanations: HTTP sends web traffic without TLS encryption.
HTTPS encrypts web traffic and helps protect confidentiality and integrity.
FTP is a file transfer protocol and is not secure by default.
SMTP is primarily used for sending email.

questions.q: Which device can block or allow traffic based on predefined security rules?
questions.explain: A firewall filters network traffic according to configured security policies and rules.
optionExplanations: A switch forwards frames within a LAN but is not primarily a policy enforcement device.
A firewall allows or blocks traffic based on defined security rules.
A hub repeats traffic to all ports and does not apply security policy.
A repeater regenerates signals rather than filtering traffic.

questions.q: The process of converting readable data into an unreadable form to protect it is called:
questions.explain: Encryption transforms plaintext into ciphertext so that only authorized parties can recover the original data using the proper key or method.
optionExplanations: Hashing creates a one-way digest and is not intended for recovering the original data.
Encryption converts readable data into protected unreadable ciphertext.
Compression reduces data size.
Encoding changes representation for compatibility, not secrecy.

questions.q: What is the main function of a VPN in security?
options: Provide encrypted communication over public networks
questions.explain: A VPN's security purpose is to protect data in transit by encrypting communications over untrusted public infrastructure.
optionExplanations: Reducing latency is not the main security function of a VPN.
VPNs often add overhead rather than increase bandwidth.
A VPN provides encrypted communication over public networks.
VLAN creation is a LAN segmentation task, not a VPN function.

questions.q: Which authentication method requires two or more verification factors?
questions.explain: Two-factor authentication requires a user to present two independent categories of proof, such as a password and a code or biometric.
optionExplanations: Single sign-on reduces repeated logins but does not inherently require multiple factors.
Two-factor authentication requires two verification factors.
Biometrics is one possible factor, not the whole multi-factor method by itself.
CAPTCHA helps distinguish humans from bots but is not the standard term for requiring multiple user identity factors.

questions.q: Which security measure helps prevent unauthorized access by identifying a user through fingerprints?
questions.explain: Fingerprint identification is a biometric method because it relies on a physical trait unique to the user.
optionExplanations: CAPTCHA tests for human interaction, not fingerprint identity.
Biometric authentication uses traits like fingerprints to verify identity.
A digital certificate authenticates systems or users cryptographically, not via fingerprint scanning.
A passphrase is knowledge-based authentication.

questions.q: In network security, what does 'least privilege' mean?
options: Users have access only to the resources needed to perform their jobs
Restricting network access to admins only
questions.explain: Least privilege means granting only the minimum access required for a user or process to perform legitimate duties.
optionExplanations: Least privilege limits access to only what is needed for the task or role.
Sharing admin rights broadly violates least privilege.
Restricting everything to admins is not the principle; access should match job need.
Encryption protects data but is separate from access-rights minimization.

questions.q: Which type of malware locks files until a ransom is paid?
questions.explain: Ransomware encrypts or otherwise blocks access to data and demands payment for restoration or decryption.
optionExplanations: A worm self-replicates across systems.
Ransomware locks or encrypts files and demands payment.
A trojan disguises itself as legitimate software.
Spyware secretly monitors user activity.

questions.q: A security analyst wants to ensure a company can recover from cyberattacks. Which process should they implement?
questions.explain: Backups and recovery procedures are essential for restoring systems and data after incidents such as ransomware or destructive attacks.
optionExplanations: Intrusion prevention helps stop attacks but is not the core recovery process.
Data backup and recovery enables restoration after a cyberattack.
Bandwidth shaping manages traffic flow, not disaster recovery.
Token passing is a media-access concept, not a recovery process.

questions.q: Which tool can be used to detect vulnerabilities in a network?
questions.explain: A network vulnerability scanner probes hosts, services, and configurations to identify weaknesses and exposures.
optionExplanations: A network scanner can discover hosts, services, and vulnerabilities for assessment.
A modem provides WAN access rather than vulnerability detection.
A switch forwards frames inside a network.
A hub broadcasts traffic and does not scan for vulnerabilities.

questions.q: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for logical addressing?
questions.explain: The OSI network layer handles logical addressing and routing, including IP addressing in common implementations.
optionExplanations: The physical layer handles signal transmission and media.
The data link layer uses physical addressing such as MAC addresses.
The network layer is responsible for logical addressing and routing.
The transport layer manages end-to-end delivery services.

questions.q: Which protocol translates domain names into IP addresses?
questions.explain: DNS resolves human-readable domain names to IP addresses so clients can locate network services.
optionExplanations: DHCP assigns IP configuration settings.
DNS translates domain names into IP addresses.
FTP transfers files.
HTTP transfers web content.

questions.q: Which protocol is used to send email messages?
questions.explain: SMTP, Simple Mail Transfer Protocol, is used to send and relay outgoing email messages.
optionExplanations: POP3 retrieves email from a server.
SMTP is the standard protocol for sending email.
IMAP is used to access and synchronize stored email.
HTTP is the web protocol, not the core mail-sending protocol.

questions.q: Which protocol securely transfers files between computers?
questions.explain: SFTP runs over SSH to provide encrypted file transfer, unlike standard FTP or TFTP.
optionExplanations: FTP transfers files but is not secure by default.
SFTP securely transfers files over an encrypted SSH connection.
TFTP is a simple file transfer protocol without strong security.
SMTP is for email transmission.

questions.q: Which protocol automatically assigns IP addresses to devices?
questions.explain: DHCP dynamically leases IP addresses and related settings such as subnet mask, gateway, and DNS servers to clients.
optionExplanations: DNS resolves names, not addresses assignment.
DHCP automatically assigns IP configuration to devices.
HTTP delivers web content.
ICMP carries control, error, and diagnostic messages.

questions.q: IPv6 addresses are represented in:
questions.explain: IPv6 notation uses hexadecimal groups separated by colons.
optionExplanations: Binary underlies all digital data, but standard written IPv6 notation is not binary.
Decimal dotted notation is used for IPv4, not IPv6.
Hexadecimal is the standard representation format for IPv6 addresses.
Octal is not the standard representation for IPv6 addresses.

questions.q: Which OSI layer ensures data is delivered error-free, in sequence, and with no losses or duplications?
questions.explain: The transport layer provides end-to-end reliability features such as sequencing, acknowledgment, retransmission, and flow control when using a protocol like TCP.
optionExplanations: The physical layer moves raw bits across media.
The network layer handles addressing and routing.
The transport layer is responsible for reliable end-to-end delivery in this description.
The session layer manages dialog control rather than core reliability guarantees.

questions.q: Which protocol is used for remote login to a server?
questions.explain: SSH provides secure remote terminal access and command execution on servers.
optionExplanations: HTTP is for web communication.
SSH is used for secure remote login and administration.
FTP transfers files rather than interactive login shells.
DHCP assigns IP settings.

questions.q: Which IEEE standard defines Wi-Fi networking?
questions.explain: IEEE 802.11 is the family of standards for wireless LANs commonly known as Wi-Fi.
optionExplanations: 802.3 defines Ethernet.
802.11 defines Wi-Fi networking.
802.15 covers personal area networking technologies such as Bluetooth-related standards.
802.5 is associated with Token Ring.

questions.q: ICMP is primarily used for:
questions.explain: ICMP supports network diagnostics and error reporting, such as ping and unreachable notifications.
optionExplanations: File sharing uses other protocols and services.
ICMP is used for diagnostics and error messages.
DHCP assigns IP addresses.
Streaming video uses application-layer protocols, not ICMP as its primary purpose.

questions.q: Which cable type is most immune to electromagnetic interference?
questions.explain: Fiber optic cable carries data as light rather than electrical signals, so it is highly immune to electromagnetic interference.
optionExplanations: UTP is more susceptible to EMI than shielded or optical media.
STP adds shielding but still uses electrical signaling.
Fiber optic is most immune to electromagnetic interference because it uses light.
Coaxial offers shielding but is still an electrical medium.

questions.q: Which device regenerates and amplifies a signal in a network?
questions.explain: A repeater restores weakened signals so they can travel farther across a network medium.
optionExplanations: A router forwards packets between networks.
A repeater regenerates or boosts a signal for extended transmission.
A hub repeats incoming traffic to all ports but is not described primarily as a signal regenerator in this context.
A firewall filters traffic.

questions.q: Which connector type is used for Ethernet cables?
questions.explain: Standard twisted-pair Ethernet patch cables typically use an 8P8C modular connector commonly referred to as RJ45.
optionExplanations: RJ45 is the common connector used for twisted-pair Ethernet cables.
RJ11 is commonly used for telephone lines.
BNC is associated with coaxial connections.
SC is a fiber optic connector type.

questions.q: A network technician needs to connect a wireless network to a wired network. Which device should they use?
questions.explain: An access point bridges wireless clients onto a wired Ethernet network, extending network access over Wi-Fi.
optionExplanations: A router connects networks and can include Wi-Fi, but the specific bridging device described is an access point.
An access point connects wireless devices to a wired network.
A switch expands wired port connectivity only.
A hub is an outdated shared-media device.

questions.q: Which cable type uses light to transmit data?
questions.explain: Fiber optic cabling carries data as pulses of light through glass or plastic strands.
optionExplanations: Fiber optic uses light to transmit data.
Coaxial uses electrical signals over copper.
UTP uses electrical signaling on twisted copper pairs.
STP also uses electrical signaling on shielded copper pairs.

questions.q: Which hardware device stores MAC addresses to forward data to the correct port?
questions.explain: A switch learns MAC addresses on its ports and forwards frames only where needed within the LAN.
optionExplanations: A switch stores learned MAC-to-port mappings and forwards frames accordingly.
A hub does not maintain MAC address tables and simply repeats traffic.
A repeater regenerates signals rather than making forwarding decisions by MAC.
A router uses IP routing between networks rather than a switch's MAC table forwarding role.

questions.q: Which tool is used to test the integrity of network cables?
questions.explain: A cable tester checks continuity, pinout, and sometimes faults such as opens, shorts, and crossed pairs in network cabling.
optionExplanations: A punch-down tool seats wires into IDC terminals.
A cable tester is used to verify cable integrity and correct pinout.
A crimping tool attaches connectors to cable ends.
A multimeter measures electrical properties but is not the standard dedicated tool for network cable integrity testing.

questions.q: Which antenna type is most suitable for long-distance point-to-point wireless communication?
questions.explain: A Yagi antenna is directional and focuses signal energy, making it useful for longer point-to-point wireless links.
optionExplanations: An omnidirectional antenna radiates broadly in many directions.
A Yagi antenna is directional and suitable for long-distance point-to-point communication.
A dipole is a basic antenna with broader pattern than a Yagi.
A patch antenna is directional but typically used for shorter, focused coverage areas than a Yagi in this context.

questions.q: Which subnet mask is commonly used for a Class C sized network with up to 254 usable host addresses?
questions.explain: A /24 mask, written as 255.255.255.0, leaves 8 host bits, allowing 254 usable IPv4 host addresses.
optionExplanations: 255.255.0.0 is a /16 and supports far more hosts.
255.255.255.0 is a /24 with 254 usable host addresses.
255.255.255.128 is a /25 with 126 usable host addresses.
255.0.0.0 is a /8 and supports many more hosts.

questions.q: What is the default port number for HTTPS?
questions.explain: HTTPS conventionally uses TCP port 443 for encrypted web traffic.
optionExplanations: Port 21 is commonly used for FTP control.
Port 22 is commonly used for SSH.
Port 80 is the default for unencrypted HTTP.
Port 443 is the default port for HTTPS.

questions.q: Which command-line utility is commonly used to test reachability between two IP devices?
questions.explain: Ping sends ICMP echo requests and checks whether a host responds, making it a basic reachability test.
optionExplanations: Ping tests basic network reachability using ICMP.
Format prepares storage media and is unrelated.
Mkdir creates directories.
Nslookup queries DNS records rather than directly testing reachability.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of NAT on a router?
options: To convert domain names to IP addresses
To map private addresses to public addresses
questions.explain: NAT allows private internal IP addresses to be translated to public addresses for communication across the internet.
optionExplanations: DNS resolves names, not NAT.
NAT maps private addresses to public addresses or other translated addresses.
Encryption is not NAT's primary purpose.
Routers using NAT do not necessarily block every inbound packet.

questions.q: Which wireless security standard is generally stronger than WPA2 because it improves authentication and protection against password guessing?
questions.explain: WPA3 improves authentication features and provides stronger protections than earlier Wi-Fi security modes.
optionExplanations: WEP is obsolete and weak.
WPA was an older improvement over WEP but is outdated.
WPA3 is the newer, stronger wireless security standard in this comparison.
Open networks provide no encryption.

questions.q: What does SSID stand for in wireless networking?
questions.explain: SSID is the network name used to identify a wireless LAN service set.
optionExplanations: This is not the meaning of SSID.
SSID stands for Service Set Identifier.
This is not a standard wireless term.
This expansion is fabricated and incorrect.

questions.q: Which device operates primarily at the Data Link layer and forwards frames based on MAC addresses?
questions.explain: A switch is a Layer 2 device that forwards Ethernet frames based on learned MAC addresses.
optionExplanations: A switch forwards frames based on MAC addresses at Layer 2.
A router primarily operates at Layer 3 using IP information.
A modem handles signal modulation and ISP connectivity.
A firewall filters traffic but is not defined by MAC-based frame switching.

questions.q: Which IPv4 address range is reserved for private addressing in the 192.168 block?
questions.explain: The RFC 1918 private 192.168 range spans 192.168.0.0 through 192.168.255.255, which is 192.168.0.0/16.
optionExplanations: 192.0.0.0 through 192.255.255.255 is much broader and not all private.
Most 172.x.x.x addresses are public; only 172.16.0.0 through 172.31.255.255 is private.
10.10.10.0/24 is private, but it is only one small subset of the 10.0.0.0/8 private range.

questions.q: Which protocol is commonly used to automatically synchronize time on networked devices?
questions.explain: NTP, Network Time Protocol, keeps clocks synchronized across systems, which is important for logs, authentication, and troubleshooting.
optionExplanations: NTP synchronizes time across networked devices.
ARP resolves IPv4 addresses to MAC addresses on a LAN.
SNMP is used for monitoring and management.
LDAP provides directory services and authentication support.

questions.q: Which record type in DNS is used to map a hostname to an IPv4 address?
questions.explain: An A record maps a hostname to an IPv4 address in DNS.
optionExplanations: MX records specify mail exchangers.
An A record maps a name to an IPv4 address.
PTR records are used for reverse lookups.
TXT records store arbitrary text data.

questions.q: Which cable standard uses the same pinout on both ends of an Ethernet patch cable for typical device-to-switch connections?
questions.explain: A straight-through Ethernet cable uses matching pinouts on both ends and is typical for unlike-device connections such as PC-to-switch.
optionExplanations: A straight-through cable uses the same pinout on both ends.
A crossover cable swaps transmit and receive pairs for certain like-device connections.
A rollover cable is commonly associated with console connections, not Ethernet patching.
Serial is not the standard Ethernet twisted-pair pinout type here.

questions.q: What does PoE allow a network cable to provide in addition to data?
questions.explain: Power over Ethernet allows compatible devices to receive power over the same twisted-pair Ethernet cable used for data.
optionExplanations: Ethernet cabling does not provide device cooling.
PoE provides electrical power along with data.
Fiber conversion requires media converters, not PoE.
PoE does not encrypt traffic.

questions.q: Which routing protocol is classified as a link-state protocol?
questions.explain: OSPF is a link-state routing protocol that builds a topology map and calculates best paths using cost.
optionExplanations: RIP is a distance-vector routing protocol.
OSPF is a link-state routing protocol.
FTP is a file transfer protocol, not a routing protocol.
ARP resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses on a local network.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a default gateway on a host?
options: To send traffic to remote networks
questions.explain: The default gateway is the router interface a host uses when sending packets to destinations outside its local subnet.
optionExplanations: A gateway does not store passwords as its core purpose.
It forwards traffic destined for remote networks outside the local subnet.
MAC addresses are assigned by hardware vendors, not gateways.
A default gateway does not terminate all local sessions.

questions.q: Which port is commonly used by DNS queries over UDP?
questions.explain: DNS commonly uses port 53, especially UDP 53 for standard queries and responses.
optionExplanations: Port 25 is SMTP.
Port 53 is the standard DNS port.
Port 110 is POP3.
Port 3389 is Remote Desktop Protocol.

questions.q: Which term describes unwanted broadcast traffic that consumes bandwidth and processing resources on a LAN?
questions.explain: A broadcast storm is excessive broadcast traffic that can overwhelm a network segment and degrade performance.
optionExplanations: A broadcast storm is excessive broadcast traffic on a LAN.
Link aggregation combines multiple links for bandwidth and redundancy.
Load balancing distributes traffic or workload across resources.
Port mirroring copies traffic for monitoring or analysis.

questions.q: What is the primary role of ARP in IPv4 networking?
options: Translate IP addresses to MAC addresses on a local network
questions.explain: ARP resolves a known IPv4 address to the corresponding MAC address so a frame can be delivered on the local LAN.
optionExplanations: DHCP assigns IP addresses automatically, not ARP.
ARP maps IPv4 addresses to MAC addresses on the local network.
ARP does not provide encryption.
Routing across networks is performed by routers, not ARP.

questions.q: Which Wi-Fi frequency band generally offers longer range but fewer non-overlapping channels than 5 GHz?
questions.explain: The 2.4 GHz band typically travels farther and penetrates obstacles better than 5 GHz, but it offers fewer clean non-overlapping channels.
optionExplanations: 2.4 GHz generally provides longer range but more congestion and fewer non-overlapping channels.
6 GHz offers more spectrum but shorter range than 2.4 GHz in typical environments.
60 GHz has very short range and poor obstacle penetration.
Infrared is not the standard Wi-Fi radio band in this comparison.

questions.q: Which tool is most commonly used to attach an RJ45 connector to twisted-pair cable?
questions.explain: A crimping tool secures an RJ45 connector onto twisted-pair conductors in a patch cable.
optionExplanations: A tone generator helps trace cables.
A crimping tool attaches modular connectors such as RJ45.
A punch-down tool seats wires into patch panels or keystone jacks.
A loopback plug is for testing interfaces, not attaching connectors.

questions.q: What is the function of a patch panel in structured cabling?
options: It centralizes cable terminations for organization and management
questions.explain: A patch panel provides an organized termination point for horizontal cabling, making moves, adds, and changes easier.
optionExplanations: Media conversion is not the role of a patch panel.
A patch panel centralizes cable terminations for cleaner management.
Traffic filtering is a firewall role.
IP address assignment is handled by DHCP, not a patch panel.

questions.q: Which statement best describes full-duplex Ethernet communication?
options: Data can move in only one direction at a time
Data can be sent and received simultaneously
Only wireless devices can use it
questions.explain: Full-duplex means a device can transmit and receive at the same time, eliminating collisions on properly switched Ethernet links.
optionExplanations: One direction at a time describes simplex, not full duplex.
Taking turns characterizes half-duplex behavior.
Full duplex allows simultaneous send and receive.
Full duplex is not limited to wireless devices.

questions.q: Which network monitoring protocol is commonly used to collect device performance data and interface statistics?
questions.explain: SNMP is widely used to query and monitor network devices for counters, status, and alerts.
optionExplanations: SNMP is used for network monitoring and management.
SMTP sends email.
SMB is a file-sharing protocol.
NFS is a network file system protocol.

questions.q: What does CIDR stand for?
questions.explain: CIDR stands for Classless Inter-Domain Routing and allows flexible network prefix lengths such as /24 or /27.
optionExplanations: This is not the recognized expansion.
CIDR stands for Classless Inter-Domain Routing.
This is not a standard networking term.
This is not the recognized expansion.

questions.q: Which device would you configure with an ACL to permit or deny specific IP traffic between networks?
questions.explain: Access control lists are commonly configured on routers and Layer 3 devices to filter traffic based on IP criteria.
optionExplanations: A router can enforce ACL rules on routed traffic.
A patch panel is passive cabling hardware.
A hub cannot apply Layer 3 filtering logic.
A cable tester is a diagnostic tool, not a packet-filtering device.

questions.q: Which network service automatically provides hostnames and IP address mappings on many local networks?
questions.explain: On many managed networks, DHCP assigns addresses while DNS maintains or updates hostname-to-address mappings so systems can be found by name.
optionExplanations: DHCP alone assigns addresses but does not by itself provide all hostname mapping functions.
DNS alone resolves names but does not automatically assign IP leases.
DHCP and DNS are commonly used together for addressing and name resolution.
FTP and TFTP are file transfer protocols.

questions.q: A user can access websites by IP address but not by domain name. Which service is the most likely problem?
questions.explain: If IP access works but names do not, name resolution is failing, which points to DNS.
optionExplanations: DNS problems commonly cause failures when domain names do not resolve but raw IP access still works.
NAT translates addresses and is not the most direct explanation here.
VLAN issues would not specifically cause name-only failure in this way.
A UPS provides backup power.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a loopback address such as 127.0.0.1?
options: To test the local TCP/IP stack on the same machine
To connect to the nearest DNS server
questions.explain: The loopback address points back to the local host and is used to test networking software without leaving the machine.
optionExplanations: A loopback address tests the local host's own TCP/IP stack.
The default gateway is usually a router interface on the subnet.
Broadcast domains are defined by switching and routing boundaries, not loopback addresses.
127.0.0.1 does not point to an external DNS server.

questions.q: Which term refers to the delay experienced by packets traveling across a network?
questions.explain: Latency is the time delay between transmission and reception or between request and response.
optionExplanations: Throughput is the amount of data transferred over time.
Latency is the delay experienced in network communication.
Topology describes arrangement of network components.
A checksum is used for error detection.

questions.q: Which technology combines multiple physical links into one logical link for higher throughput or redundancy?
questions.explain: Link aggregation bundles multiple interfaces so they operate as one logical connection for added bandwidth and or resilience.
optionExplanations: Link aggregation combines multiple physical links into one logical link.
Token passing is a media access method.
Port scanning probes services on hosts.
Subnetting divides address space into smaller networks.

questions.q: What is the main advantage of fiber optic backbone cabling in an enterprise building?
options: It is easier to tap without detection
It supports high bandwidth over longer distances
It is affected more by EMI
questions.explain: Fiber is favored for backbones because it supports high data rates over longer distances and resists electromagnetic interference.
optionExplanations: Fiber is not easier to tap securely; that would be a disadvantage, not an advantage.
Fiber supports high bandwidth over longer distances, ideal for backbones.
Fiber installations still require optical transceivers or interfaces.
Fiber is less affected by EMI, not more.

questions.q: Which protocol securely encrypts remote command-line administration traffic?
questions.explain: SSH encrypts remote shell sessions, unlike Telnet which sends data in plaintext.
optionExplanations: Telnet is plaintext and insecure by modern standards.
SSH securely encrypts remote command-line administration.
HTTP is for web traffic.
SNMPv1 lacks modern security and is not a remote shell protocol.

questions.q: Which IPv4 private address block begins with 10?
questions.explain: The 10.0.0.0/8 block is one of the RFC 1918 private IPv4 address ranges.
optionExplanations: 10.0.0.0/8 is the private 10.x.x.x address block.
10.0.0.0/24 is only one subnet inside the larger private /8 range.
100.64.0.0/10 is shared address space used by carriers, not standard RFC 1918 private space.
11.0.0.0/8 is public space.

questions.q: What is the primary reason to disable unused switch ports?
questions.explain: Unused active ports can provide unauthorized physical access opportunities, so disabling them improves security.
optionExplanations: Monitor size is unrelated to switch port configuration.
Disabling unused ports reduces the attack surface and limits unauthorized access points.
Screen brightness is unrelated.
DNS is unaffected by disabling unused switch ports.

questions.q: Which wireless network design practice helps reduce co-channel interference in an office?
options: Using the same channel on every access point
Careful channel planning across nearby access points
questions.explain: Selecting channels strategically across nearby APs reduces interference and improves wireless performance.
optionExplanations: Using the same channel everywhere increases contention and interference.
Channel planning helps nearby APs avoid overlapping excessively and reduces co-channel interference.
Encryption settings do not solve channel overlap.
Packet size is not the primary control for co-channel interference.

questions.q: Which address is used to send a frame to all devices on an Ethernet LAN segment?
questions.explain: A broadcast address targets all hosts on the local broadcast domain or all devices on a LAN segment for the frame type in question.
optionExplanations: A unicast address targets one destination.
A broadcast address targets all devices in the broadcast domain or segment.
A loopback address points back to the same host.
Anycast is not the Ethernet all-hosts addressing concept here.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of a DMZ in network design?
options: To place public-facing services in a separate network zone
To increase battery life of laptops
To convert IPv6 to IPv4 automatically
questions.explain: A DMZ isolates internet-facing servers from the internal LAN to reduce risk if those public services are compromised.
optionExplanations: A DMZ separates public-facing systems from the internal network for security.
Battery life is unrelated to DMZ design.
IPv6 to IPv4 conversion is not the main purpose of a DMZ.
A DMZ complements firewall design rather than replacing all firewalls.

questions.q: Which type of IP address assignment remains manually configured and does not change unless an administrator edits it?
questions.explain: A static IP address is manually configured and stays the same until changed by an administrator.
optionExplanations: A dynamic address may change based on DHCP leasing.
A static address is manually configured and remains fixed until changed.
APIPA is automatic self-assignment when DHCP fails, not deliberate manual addressing.
A leased address refers to dynamic DHCP behavior.

questions.q: If a workstation shows a 169.254.x.x address, what is the most likely issue?
options: It successfully contacted the DHCP server
It assigned itself an APIPA address because DHCP failed
It is using a valid public address
questions.explain: An address in 169.254.0.0/16 usually indicates APIPA self-assignment after the host could not obtain a DHCP lease.
optionExplanations: This usually indicates DHCP did not succeed, not that it succeeded.
The host self-assigned an APIPA address because it could not reach or obtain service from DHCP.
APIPA is an IPv4 behavior, not an IPv6-only indication.
169.254.x.x is not a normal public internet address assignment.

questions.q: What is the function of a MAC address on a NIC?
options: It provides a physical layer voltage
It identifies the network interface at Layer 2
It assigns domain names to hosts
It replaces the need for IP
questions.explain: A MAC address is the hardware-based identifier used for local frame delivery on Ethernet and similar Layer 2 networks.
optionExplanations: Voltage signaling is not the purpose of a MAC address.
A MAC address identifies the interface at the data link layer.
DNS assigns domain names, not MAC addresses.
IP is still needed for routed communication beyond the local segment.

questions.q: Which command is most appropriate for displaying the path packets take to a remote host?
questions.explain: Tracert or traceroute reveals the sequence of router hops a packet traverses toward a destination.
optionExplanations: Tracert shows the path and hops to a remote host.
Mkdir creates directories.
Copy duplicates files.
Arp -d deletes ARP cache entries and does not trace routes.

questions.q: Which network documentation item shows how devices, links, and addressing fit together visually?
questions.explain: A topology diagram visually documents devices, connections, and often addressing or logical segmentation.
optionExplanations: A topology diagram shows network devices and their interconnections.
An acceptable use policy is a rules document for users.
A help desk ticket records an issue or request.
A password vault stores credentials.

questions.q: Which port is typically used for SMTP email submission or relay in basic exam contexts?
questions.explain: SMTP is commonly associated with port 25 in basic networking exam questions, though other submission ports also exist in practice.
optionExplanations: 53 is DNS.
80 is HTTP.
25 is the classic SMTP port tested in many networking contexts.
161 is SNMP.

questions.q: What does a UPS provide to critical network equipment during a power outage?
questions.explain: A UPS supplies temporary backup power so network devices can continue running or shut down gracefully during an outage.
optionExplanations: Roaming is a wireless client mobility function.
A UPS provides backup power during outages.
DNS resolution is unrelated to UPS hardware.
A UPS does not allocate public IP addresses.

questions.q: Which practice best protects switch management interfaces from unauthorized remote access?
options: Using strong credentials and limiting management access
Enabling every service on the switch
questions.explain: Strong authentication and restricted management access reduce the chance of administrative compromise.
optionExplanations: Default passwords are a serious security risk.
Strong credentials and restricted management access are best practice.
Enabling unnecessary services increases the attack surface.
Publicizing management details weakens security.

questions.q: Which statement about hubs is true?
options: They forward traffic only to the intended port
They create separate collision domains per port
They repeat incoming traffic out all ports
questions.explain: A hub is a Layer 1 device that repeats traffic to all ports, unlike a switch that forwards selectively.
optionExplanations: Selective forwarding is a switch behavior, not a hub behavior.
Hubs do not create separate collision domains per port.
Hubs repeat incoming traffic to all other ports.
Routing between VLANs requires Layer 3 capability, not a hub.

questions.q: What is the purpose of inter-VLAN routing?
options: To allow communication between different VLANs
questions.explain: Because VLANs are separate Layer 2 broadcast domains, Layer 3 routing is needed for hosts in different VLANs to communicate.
optionExplanations: Inter-VLAN routing enables communication between hosts on different VLANs.
Media conversion is unrelated.
Wireless signal amplification is unrelated.
DNS is a separate network service.

questions.q: Which metric describes how much data can be transferred across a link per second?
questions.explain: Bandwidth refers to the capacity of a network link, commonly expressed in bits per second.
optionExplanations: Bandwidth is the data-carrying capacity of a link.
Latency is delay.
Jitter is variation in delay.
Uptime is the duration a system remains operational.

questions.q: Which security concept separates guest Wi-Fi users from internal corporate resources?
questions.explain: Network segmentation isolates guest traffic from internal systems, limiting access and reducing risk.
optionExplanations: Network segmentation isolates groups of devices and access levels.
Overclocking affects hardware speed, not access separation.
Port mirroring copies traffic for analysis.
Token Ring is an older networking technology, not the security concept here.

questions.q: Which protocol version provides a much larger address space than IPv4?
questions.explain: IPv6 uses 128-bit addressing, providing vastly more addresses than IPv4's 32-bit scheme.
optionExplanations: IPv2 is not the modern address-space expansion successor.
IPv5 was not adopted as the internet addressing standard.
IPv6 provides a much larger address space than IPv4.
IPX is a different historical protocol suite.

questions.q: Which troubleshooting step should come first when a user's wired PC has no network connection?
options: Replace every switch in the building
Check the physical link, cable, and NIC status
Change the company's public IP block
questions.explain: Troubleshooting should begin with basic physical checks such as cable connection, link lights, and NIC status before more disruptive actions.
optionExplanations: Replacing all switches is excessive and not the first step.
Checking physical connectivity and NIC status is the correct first troubleshooting step.
Reinstalling the OS is far too drastic as an initial action.
Changing the public IP block is unrelated to a single PC's first-line troubleshooting.
